blocks.text: ASPECT GRAPH GENERATION FOR NON-CONVEX
POLYHEDRA FROM PERSPECTIVE PROJECTION VIEW
I-Cheng Chang and Chung-Lin Huang †
Electrical Engineering Department, National Tsing-Hua University, Hsin-Chu, Taiwan, Republic of China
(Received 15 July 1991; in revised form 8 January 1992; received for publication 14 February 1992)
Abstract-The aspect graph is basically a multiple-view representation of the designated three-dimensional
(3D) object. In this paper, a complete algorithm is presented for constructing the aspect graph of general
polyhedral objects from the perspective point of view. The aspect graph generation is based on a so-called
visual event. These visual events will completely capture the way the appearance of an edge or a face can
be occluded by other faces and edges in the model. All the visual events can be found for polyhedra as facial,
\( E E \), and \( E E E \) events. The facial event indicates the visibility transition of the faces, the \( E E \) event indicates
the possible visibility variation of the occlusion of two edges, and the \( E E E \) event represents another visibility
change, the consecutive occlusion among three edges. The system consists of the following five stages:
(1) find the viewing cell for each planar face, \( E E \) event, and \( E E E \) event and then create the "parcellation
graph"; (2) employ the parcellation graph to create a "face-level enumeration array" and "edge-level
enumeration array"; (3) generate test points; (4) examine plausible cells that enclose a volume of viewing
space; and (5) generate an explicit aspect graph structure. Finally, nine non-convex polyhedra are obtained
in the experiments. The aspect graphs of these non-regular shaped objects can be easily generated by this
system.
Aspect graph Non-convex polyhedral Viewing cell Facial event \( E E \) event \( E E E \) event
Parcellation graph Enumeration array
1. INTRODUCTION
The effective object representation of the three-dimen
sional (3D) objects is an essential problem for the major
research area of 3D object recognition. Three-dimen
sional object recognition is basically a matching problem
between the two-dimensional (2D) image information
and the prestored model. The prestored model can be
either a 3D object model or multiple 2D views of the
objects. Here, we introduce an algorithm to generate
effective multiple 2D views of the 3D objects so that the
recognition process is reduced to a much simpler 2D to
2D matching problem. The idea behind this approach
is that if a stored view is similar to the image, the
recognition process will be simple and fast. The set of
2D views, which represent the infinite number of
possible views, need to be as small as possible to
minimize the number of views that must be stored.
Therefore, the views are generally taken to be topologi
cally distinct.
This paper is concerned with the creation of aspect
graphs for use as object models in a 3D object
recognition system. Koenderink and van Doorn \( { }^{(1)} \)
introduced the notion of an aspect graph for represent
ing object shape. An aspect is a qualitatively distinct
view of an object as seen from connected viewpoints in
the viewpoint space. We will develop a method for
directly creating the aspect graph of a polyhedral
† Author to whom all correspondence should be addressed.
object from its boundary surface representation. Each
node in the graph has attributes that describe one
aspect of the object and the viewing cell from which
that aspect can be viewed. Each arc in the graph
indicates an adjacency between two aspects and the
plane, which separates the two associated viewing cells.
The aspect graph representation combines an exact
3D model of an object with a set of generic views. Each
generic view is associated with a viewing cell of space
from which it can be seen. Thus, the aspect graph is a
hybrid representation, which combines the complete
ness of a viewpoint-independent representation with
the simplicity of a viewpoint-dependent representation.
The aspect graph of an object is a graph structure in
which (1) each node of the graph, or aspect, represents a
topologically distinct view of the object as seen from
some maximal, connected region of viewpoint space,
and there is a node for each aspect; (2) each arc
represents a visual event, which occurs at the transition
from one region of the viewpoint space to a neighboring
region, and there is an arc for each such transition.
There are two approaches to partition the viewing
space: (1) uniform object-independent partition and (2)
aspect graph approach. The viewing space has been
modeled as either Gaussian sphere, resulting in an
orthographic projection aspect, or as all 3D space,
resulting in a perspective projection aspect. In the first
approach the viewing space is partitioned uniformly by
projecting a tessellated regular polyhedron onto the
sphere. The tessellation of the polyhedron and the
partition of the regions on the surface of the sphere is
predetermined and independent of the structures.
Then, the neighboring regions that have the same
aspect are combined. \( { }^{(2-4)} \) The uniform partition has to
be fine enough to avoid missing any of the aspects. The
advantage of this approach is that the partition is easy
to compute and fix for all objects. The difficulty is in
selecting the right scale of the partition. Under a fine
partition, the views in many neighboring regions are
qualitatively similar. On the other hand, under coarse
partition some important views might be missing.
In the second approach, the viewing space, as
proposed by Koenderink and van Doorn, \( { }^{(1)} \) is parti
tioned according to the qualitative structure of the
view, which is called the aspect. An aspect may be
defined in one of several ways, depending on the
application at hand. The aspect may be defined by the
graph structure of the line drawing, by the set of visible
faces, by the set of occluding edges, or by the other
structure of the view. They describe a graph structure
called the "visual potential" of the object. Chakravarty
and Freeman describe an approach to object recogni
tion, which uses similar concepts called "characteristic
views". \( { }^{(5)} \) Some previous work has been published on
constructing the aspect graph.
Based on different viewing methods (orthographic
or perspective projections) as well as different viewing
objects (convex or non-convex objects), we may classify
the previous aspect graph generation methods into
four categories:
(1) Perspective-convex. Stewman and Bowyer \( { }^{(6)} \) first
proposed a solution for generating the aspect graphs of
the planar-face objects from the perspective view.
Gaultieri et al. \( { }^{(7)} \) showed how to construct an aspect
graph for convex polyhedra with the viewpoint con
strained to lie in the plane containing the polygon,
using perspective projections. Watts \( { }^{(8)} \) described an
\( O\left(n^{4}\right) \)-time algorithm for enumerating the "principal
views" of convex polyhedra.
(2) Perspective-non-convex. Stewman and Bowyer, \( { }^{(9)} \)
in their later paper, described an algorithm for con
structing the viewing data of convex polyhedra under
perspective projection. Wang and Freeman \( { }^{(10)} \) pre
sented a method to partition the viewing space of a
planar-faced solid into characteristic view domains
and found the set of characteristic views of the solid.
(3) Orthographic-convex. Mckenna and Seidel \( { }^{(11)} \)
constructed minimal and maximal shadows of convex
polyhedra in the plane using the \( O\left(n^{2}\right) \) algorithm.
Edelsbrunner et al. \( { }^{(12)} \) developed similar methods.
(4) Orthographic-non-convex. Gigus and co
workers \( { }^{(13,14)} \) developed an algorithm for computing
the aspect graph for polyhedral objects under ortho
graphic projection. Plantinga and Dyer \( { }^{(15)} \) also showed
how to construct the aspect graph. Their algorithm
computes the partition together with a representative
view for each region of the partition.
When dealing with the convex polyhedra, the parcel
lation of viewing space is entirely determined by the
planes on which the face of the object lies. The major
complication in handling the non-convex polyhedra is
the possibility of self-occlusion. Stewman and Bowyer \( { }^{(9)} \)
introduced the auxiliary surfaces for parcellation of the
viewing space of the non-convex polyhedra. An auxil
iary plane is defined by the visual interaction of a vertex
and an edge of the object. Wang and Freeman \( { }^{(10)} \) fol
lowed the same algorithm and renamed the three
parcellation surfaces as type A, type B, and type C
surfaces. However, the parcellation surfaces (types B
and C ) are not sufficient for describing all \( E E \) and \( E E E \)
event transitions. Here, we analyze all the possible
occurrences of \( E E \) and \( E E E \) events and develop
algorithms for constructing their corresponding viewing
cells. Our approach is different from previous methods
in developing a robust and comprehensive aspect
graph generation algorithm.
2. FACIAL EVENT AND EE EVENT
One of the major steps in aspect graph generation is
to find the occurrences of singularities. The singularities
are determined by three basic events: facial events, \( E E \)
events, and \( E E E \) events. The facial event occurs when
the viewer changes his viewpoints and the entire face
becomes invisible. Also, when a T-junction appears
when edge A occludes edge B or not, the \( E E \) event
occurs. When the \( E E E \) event occurs, there will be three
edges which occlude one another. A detailed description
about the three events will be described later. In this
section, we develop several concepts for finding the
viewing cell in 3D viewpoint space from which the
specific event (facial or \( E E \) event) can be observed. The
differences between our approaches and the others are
that we do a complete categorization of different types
of \( E E \) events and use them to handle more complex
non-convex objects. In the next section, we study \( E E E \)
events and add these events to our system. Before
mentioning the facial and \( E E \) events, we define some
phrases that will be used throughout this paper.
Visual event. A visual event is a viewpoint where the
connectivity, or the topology, of the projected line
drawing changes. The visual event arises when the
viewpoint moves from one aspect to another aspect.
Viewing cell. It is a set of continuous viewpoints in the
viewing space from which the same aspect is observed.
The range of the viewing cell for an event depends on
the definition of the aspect.
Expanding plane. The plane in which one face lies on is
called the expanding plane for that face.
Basic plane ( \( B P \) ). The expanding plane of the object
face is called the basic plane of the object.
Auxiliary plane (AP). The auxiliary plane is a plane
generated by a vertex and an edge of an object.
Viewing subspace. The viewing subspace of a facial
event, \( E E \) event or \( E E E \) event is the union of viewpoints,
from which we may see the face, the T-junction of the
Fig. 1. The inside/outside parts of plane A.
edge pair, or the combination of two T-junctions
formed by three edges.
An expanding plane of one face will separate the
space into two parts: one part on the positive side of the
plane is called outside the plane, and the other part on
the negative side of the plane is called inside the plane.
Figure 1 shows the inside and outside parts of a plane.
In a convex object case, the viewing cells can be
obtained through cutting the viewing space by the
basic plane. This convex case is simple because no
occlusion occurs. For a convex object, the viewpoint
lies outside a face indicating that the face is visible for
this viewpoint, whereas for the non-convex object, it is
not necessarily true. In the non-convex case, even if the
viewpoint lies outside the face, the face may still be
invisible because it may be occluded by another face of
the object. Here we introduce a partitioning plane
called an auxiliary plane (AP), and take it with BP to
partition the viewing space of a non-convex object.
Figure 2 shows the basic plane and the auxiliary plane.
2.1. Facial event
A face can be visible, partly occluded, or invisible. In
this paper, a facial event indicates the visibility changes
of a face. The face event occurs when the face visibility
changes from visible to partial occluded, or invisible.
The T-junction is observed when the face is partially
occluded. For the viewpoint in the viewing subspace of
the facial event, the face is completely visible or
partially visible, whereas for a viewpoint in the viewing
cell of the facial event, the face must be completely
visible. Therefore, if the viewpoint is not in the viewing
cell of the facial event, the face may still be partially
visible. In the \( E E \) event analysis, we may find the subset
of the viewing subspace of the facial event, where the
face is partially visible.
The basic goal of our algorithm is to differentiate the
viewing subspace of the facial event from the viewing
subspace of the \( E E \) event where the T-junction may
occur. The facial event viewing cell is obtained by
subtracting the potential \( E E \) event viewing subspaces
from its original viewing subspace. There may be more
than one potential \( E E \) viewing subspace generated.
The potential \( E E \) event viewing subspaces are generated
by the following steps:
Step 1. Find the faces that may occlude the target
face as potential occluding faces.
Step 2. Make two or more potential occluding faces
that are adjacent as a face group.
Step 3. Generate the viewing subspace for each po
tential \( E E \) event from the intersection of the inside
subspaces of the BPs belonging to the same face group.
The viewing cell of the facial event for target face \( F \)
can be represented as equation (1). The advantage of
using the viewing cell instead of the viewing subspace
for the facial event is to improve the speed of the aspect
graph generation process, to avoid the exhaustive
searching for \( E E \) events, and to reduce the computing
time. It will be explained in more detail in Section 4.
Figure 3 shows an example for the facial event of face \( F \).
\( R_{\mathrm{F}}=R_{\mathrm{OF}}-R_{\mathrm{CF}} \quad \) and
where \( R_{\mathrm{F}} \) is the viewing cell of the facial event for face F ;
\( R_{\mathrm{OF}} \) (the outside part of face F\( )=\{p=(x, y, z) \mid
\left.\left(p-p_{0}(\mathrm{~F})\right) \cdot \vec{n}_{\mathrm{F}}>0\right\}, R_{\mathrm{IF}} \) (the inside part of face F\( )=
\left\{p=(x, y, z) \mid\left(p-p_{0}(\mathrm{~F})\right) \cdot \vec{n}_{\mathrm{F}} \leq 0\right\}, p_{0}(\mathrm{~F}) \) is a vertex of
face \( \mathrm{F}, \vec{n}_{\mathrm{F}} \) the surface normal of face \( \mathrm{F}, p_{0}(j) \) a vertex of
face \( j, \vec{n}_{j} \) the surface normal of face \( j, m i \) the number of
faces which belong to face group \( i \) and \( n \) the number of
face groups.
2.2. EE event
The \( E E \) event occurs when two edges occlude each
other and the T-junction is visible. In this section we
will introduce a method to find the viewing cell of each
\( E E \) event. The T-junction occurs because of the oc
clusion of two edges, and the viewing subspace for this
\( E E \) event may be intercepted by other faces. Therefore,
Fig. 2. BPs and APs for an object.
Fig. 3. An example for the facial event of the target face-face A.
this T-junction may be partially visible or totally
invisible. The interception problem of the \( E E \) event is a
troublesome subject and it makes the \( E E \) event more
complex. Generally speaking, the types of interception
may be classified into three cases: (1) no interception,
(2) complete interception, (3) partial interception. First,
we introduce some definitions of the phrases that are
used for \( E E \) event interpretations.
Non-occluding face (or convex face). If the extending
plane of a face has the entire object lie on one side of the
plane, then we call this face a non-occluding face. For
convex objects, all the faces are non-occluding faces.
Occluding face (or concave face). If a face is not a
non-occluding face, then it is an occluding face.
Non-occluding edge. If the two intersecting faces of an
edge are all non-occluding faces, then this edge is
termed a non-occluding edge.
Occluding edge. An edge is called an occluding edge if
at least one of the two intersecting faces of this edge is
an occluding face.
Occlusion number. The number of intersecting faces of
an edge that are occlusion faces is defined as the
occlusion number of this edge.
Figure 4 illustrates the geometrical configuration of
Fig. 5. Possible existence condition and single-face complete
interception.
the definitions. From the geometric properties, the \( E E \)
event will not occur if one of the two edges is a
non-occluding edge. Since non-occluding edges are
located on the extreme boundary of an object, they will
not occlude with other edges. Therefore, the necessary
condition for generating the \( E E \) event is that both edges
are occluding edges.
(1) Possible existence condition and single-face com
plete interception
The necessary condition for the existence of the \( E E \)
event is that the two edges must not be parallel.
Therefore, the parallel edge pairs generate no \( E E \)
events and have no viewing cells. After finding the
possible existence of the \( E E \) event, we check the
single-face complete interception. If one edge is located
completely in the subspace bounded by the intersecting
planes of the other edge, the possible existence \( E E \)
event is single-face complete interception.
The testing criteria for the possible existence condi
tion and single-face complete interception are illustrated
in equations (2) and (3). Their geometrical properties of
the single-face complete intercepted condition are
demonstrated in Fig. 5.
Possible existence condition:

where \( \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \) are two vertices of one edge; \( \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d} \) two vertices
of the other edge; \( \vec{n}_{1}, \vec{n}_{2} \) the surface normals of the
plane adjacent to the latter edge; and \( \vec{n}_{3}, \vec{n}_{4} \) the surface
normals of the plane adjacent to the previous edge.
From Fig. 5, we can find that the faces adjacent to edge
ab are planes A and B and the faces adjacent to edge cd
are planes C and D . Vertices c and d are not in the
outside region of planes \( \mathbf{A} \) and \( \mathbf{B} \), this edge pair is
single-face completely intercepted. In other words, \( E E \)
events of edge ab vs. edge cd, or edge cf vs. edge ag are
invisible. An edge pair that satisfies the possible
existence testing condition but is not single-face com
pletely intercepted, does necessarily generate the \( E E \)
event.
(2) Non-intercepted \( E E \) event finding criteria
We develop some criteria to find the \( E E \) events that
are not intercepted. To illustrate the non-intercepted
\( E E \) event finding criteria (see Fig. 6), we assume: (1) two
edges \( \mathrm{e}_{1}, \mathrm{e}_{2} \); (2) viewpoint v ; (3) surface normals \( n_{1}, n_{2} \),
terminals of \( e_{1} \), points \( c \) and \( d \) are terminals of \( e_{2} \).
(1) Only one of the two intersecting faces of \( \mathrm{e}_{1} \) (i.e.
\( f_{1} \) and \( f_{2} \) ) may be seen in this aspect, i.e.

\[
(v-a) \cdot n_{1}>0 \quad \text { and } \quad(v-a) \cdot n_{2}<0
\]
or
(2) Viewpoint v is inside the region bounded by the
four planar sides of the tetrahedron, i.e.

cad, acd, adb may not be intercepted by any faces. Let
\( P_{1}, P_{2}, P_{3} \) and \( P_{4} \) be any points on line segments \( a c, b c \),

conditions:

equation \( \mathrm{A}_{i} x+\mathrm{B}_{i} y+\mathrm{C}_{i} z=\mathrm{D}_{i} \) and vertices \( \left.\mathrm{V}_{i, j}\right), \mathrm{P}_{k} \)
must be located outside of the face \( f_{i} \).
(3) Background edge's intersecting faces visibility
finding criteria
When two edges form a T-junction, we see only one
of the intersecting faces of the foreground edge. How
ever for the two intersecting faces of the background
edge, we may observe both or only one of them. The
visibility of the background edge's intersecting faces
are identified by the following two criteria:
(1) One of the two intersecting faces of \( e_{2} \) (i.e. \( f_{3} \) and
\( f_{4} \) ) is seen in this aspect, i.e.

\[
(\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{c}) \cdot n_{3}>0 \quad \text { and } \quad(\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{c}) \cdot n_{4}<0
\]
or
(2) If both of the intersecting faces of \( e_{2} \) (i.e. \( f_{3} \) and \( f_{4} \) )
are seen in this aspect then
By extending the two intersecting faces of the
background edge ( \( f_{3} \) and \( f_{4} \) ) as two half-planes, we may
divide the extended tetrahedron into as many as three
subspaces. This division indicates that the \( E E \) event
cell can be partitioned to three sub-cells (upper, mid
dle, and lower sub-cells). In Fig. 7, we can see three
sub-cells marked as I, II, and III. In the upper and lower
sub-cells, only one of the two intersecting faces of the
occluded edge is visible, whereas, in the middle sub-cell,
both of the two faces are visible. Not every \( E E \) event cell
Fig. 6. The denotations.
Fig. 7. The \( E E \) event cell may be subdivided into three sub-cells that may capture either one or both of the
intersecting faces of the background edge.
can be partitioned into as many as three sub-cells.
Some \( E E \) event cells can only be partitioned into two
sub-cells, some cannot be partitioned at all. It depends
on the interception between the extended half-plane
and the extended tetrahedron.
(4) Edge pair categorization
Here we introduce two kinds of categorization,
occlusion type and interception type, for each edge pair
that belongs to a possible existence \( E E \) event, but not a
single-face complete interception.
(a) Occlusion type
The characteristics of two intersecting faces of an
edge will deeply influence the condition of the \( E E \) event.
The two intersecting faces of any edge can be occluding
faces. The number of faces that are occluding faces is
defined as the occlusion number of this edge. Figure 8
shows the four types with el as the background edge
and e 2 as the foreground edge. Occlusion number will
be used to classify edge pairs into four occlusion types:
types I, II, III, and IV (see Table 1).
(b) Interception type
Besides occlusion type, we still need to investigate
the interception phenomenon between each pair of
edges. They may be completely or partially intercepted
by single or multiple faces. They may not be occluded at
all. This kind of non-interception case can be handled
by non-intercepted \( E E \) event finding criteria. However,
the line of sight can still be intercepted by faces outside
the tetrahedron which is bounded by four faces that are
generated by connecting the four edge terminals.
There are nine interception types separated into two

groups. The first group consists of types A, B, C, and D,
and the second group consists of types \( \mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{G}, \mathrm{H} \), and I .
The first group is involved with the possible interception
between two edges. The second group is related with
the possible interception outside the tetrahedron which
is bounded by four faces that are generated by connect
ing the four terminals of these two edges. Each edge
pair may have two attributes, one comes from the first
group, and the other one comes from the second group.
Types E-I are involved with the occurrence of the
\( E E E \) event and will be discussed in the next section. In
the following, we introduce four interception types:
Type A. Type A denotes that there is no interception
between two edges. For types I-IV, the interception
condition may occur (see Fig. 9).
Type B. Type B denotes that there exists a single
face partial interception between two edges. This type
is more complicated than type A. If we use the same
method as type A, we will find that the T-junction is not
visible for all the viewpoints inside the viewing subspace.
An example of type B is shown in Fig. 10. From Fig. 10,
we find that line segments bc and bd pass through face
A and the tetrahedron is partially intercepted by face A .
The viewpoint located at point V1, ed1, is invisible
because the viewing direction is intercepted by face A.
The T-junction formed by ed1 and ed2 is not found. But
when the viewpoint moves to point V2, the T-junction
appears. So, we should cut the T-junction invisible
viewing region from the original viewing subspace.
Type C. Type C denotes that there exists a multi
face partial interception between two edges. In the
multi-face interception case, the tetrahedron will be
intercepted by the observed object itself. Figure 11
shows an example of this type. From Fig. 11, line
segments ac and bc cross faces A and C . The intercepting
faces of this edge pair are faces \( \mathrm{A}-\mathrm{C} \). We handle the case
through the following steps:
Step 1. Find the edges, part of them are located
inside the tetrahedron.
Step 2. Create the APs by connecting the terminals
of each inside edge and terminals of edge 1. In Fig. 11,
the APs are (pt1,pt4,a), (pt2,pt3,a), (pt1,pt4,b), and
(pt2,pt3,b). "(pt1, pt4, a)" is the AP that passes three
points pt1, pt4, and a. The negative side of each AP is
intercepted by faces A-C.
Step 3. Union the inside part of all APs.
Step 4. Remove the union part from the original
viewing subspace.
Fig. 9. Examples of type A.
Fig. 10. An example of type B.
Fig. 11. An example of type C.
Fig. 12. An example of type D.
Type D. Type D denotes that there exists multi-face
complete interception between two edges. In this
interception type, T-junction is invisible. Type D is
similar to type C, except that the two edges are fully
separated by intercepting faces in type D. An example
is shown in Fig. 12. Figure 12 shows that ad, ac, bd, and
bc all pass through face A and face C . There is no
viewing subspace of the type \( D \) edge pair. In other
words, when we find that all the line segments are
intercepted by several faces, the edge pair belongs to
type D.
Using these two characteristics for a pair of edges, we

define a three-tuple representation for each edge pair:

this combination exists, and "-" means that this

(5) The relation between the viewing cells of the facial
event and the \( E E \) event
The viewing subspace for the facial event of face \( j \) is
defined as the union of viewpoints from which face \( j \) is
visible. However, in this viewing subspace, from some
viewpoints we see the entire face and no occlusion,
whereas, from other viewpoints, we see the face being
partially occluded by some other faces ( \( E E \) event
occurs). So the viewpoints belonging to the same
subspace of the facial event may generate different
aspects. In order to differentiate those different aspects,
we need to address the subspace of the facial event.
Suppose the viewing subspace of face A is as shown
in Fig. 13. The viewing subspace of the \( E E_{1,2} \) event for
ed1 and ed2 (ed1 is the background edge and ed2 the
foreground edge) is a continuous set of viewing points
from which a T-junction is observed. Therefore, the
viewing subspace of the facial event for face A can be
divided into several viewing subspaces of the \( E E \) event
(i.e. \( E E_{1,2}, E E_{3,9}, E E_{1,3} \), and \( E E_{1,4} \) ) and the viewing cell
of the facial event. If no \( E E E \) event exists, the viewing
cell of any \( E E \) event will be equal to its viewing
subspace. In Fig. 13, the boundary of the viewing cell or
the viewing subspace is the enumeration of the view
points where the specific singularity is observed. The
region enveloped by \( \mathrm{ab}, \mathrm{bc}, \mathrm{cd} \), and da is the viewing cell
of the facial event. If the viewpoint is located in this
region, face A can be seen completely. The region
enveloped by ab, bf, fe, ea is the viewing subspace of the
\( E E_{1,3} \) event (background edge is ed1 and foreground
edge is ed3). If the viewpoint is in the region cgh, both
T-junctions belong to the \( E E_{3,5} \) event and the \( E E_{1,2} \)
events are all visible. For the event which occurs in ab,
it is called a facial event when the viewpoint passes from
region abcd to region aefb. It may be called an \( E E \) event
when the viewpoint passes from region aefb to region
abcd. Therefore, if the boundary of one cell overlay the
boundary of another viewing cell, then the event of the
boundary is decided by the direction of transition of the
viewpoint.
3. EEE EVENT
For non-convex objects, only the facial event and the
\( E E \) event are not enough to represent all the aspects.
There may be some other edges existing in the viewing
subspace of the \( E E \) event that incur in the \( E E E \) event.
The viewing cell of the EEE event is the intersection set
of two or more viewing subspaces of the \( E E \) events. The
three \( E E \) events of three mutually occluded edges is a
necessary but not a sufficient condition for generating
the \( E E E \) event. For instance, in Fig. 14 there are three
Fig. 13. The relation between the viewing cells of the facial event and the \( E E \) events.
Fig. 14. Three \( E E \) events without generating the \( E E E \) event because no intersection in the viewing space is
found.
\( E E \) events which have no intersections in the viewing
space, they are not qualified to be called an \( E E E \) event.
The EEE event cells are identified under the following
criteria:
(1) the foreground edge \( \left(e_{1}\right) \) occludes the middle
ground edge ( \( \mathrm{e}_{2} \) ) as well as the background edge ( \( \mathrm{e}_{3} \) );
(2) the middle-ground edge ( \( e_{2} \) ) occludes the back
ground edge ( \( \mathrm{e}_{3} \) );
(3) the interception occurs between the two \( E E \)
event cells from edges ( \( \mathrm{e}_{1}, \mathrm{e}_{2} \) ) and ( \( \mathrm{e}_{1}, \mathrm{e}_{3} \) );
(4) the interception occurs between the two \( E E \)
event cells from edges ( \( \mathrm{e}_{1}, \mathrm{e}_{2} \) ) and ( \( \mathrm{e}_{2}, \mathrm{e}_{3} \) ).
The example illustrated in Fig. 14 only satisfies the first
two criteria. They are three unrelated \( E E \) events. We
see only one T-junction at any instance. Three non
adjacent edges that miss either one of the last two
criteria are related to the double- \( E E \) event.
There are three types of \( E E E \) events (types I-III),
double- \( E E \) event, and preceding- \( E E \) event illustrated
in Fig. 15. Two of the \( E E E \) events (types I and II) are
Fig. 15. We illustrate different configurations of \( E E E \) events, double- \( E E \) events, preceding- \( E E \) events, and
\( E E \) events, and the different transitions among them.
projected similar to the double- \( E E \) event and the
preceding-EE event. That is because of the occlusion of
the T -junction made by the middle-ground edge ( \( \mathrm{e}_{2} \) )
and background edges ( \( \mathrm{e}_{3} \) ). No occlusion occurs for the
three T-junctions in the type III \( E E E \) event that makes
it explicitly different from double- \( E E \) or preceding
\( E E \) events. The transitions from one \( E E E \) event to the
other \( E E E \) event go through an \( E E E \) singularity which
is determined by three edges. These three edges are
projected to intersect at the same image point which
may or may not be occluded by the foreground face.
This singularity can be seen on the so-called type C
surface. \( { }^{(10)} \) The type C surface is formed by all the view
ing positions that project three T vertices on the same
image point. It is composed of infinitely many straight
lines satisfying the condition that they all pass through
the three edges \( \mathrm{e}_{1}, \mathrm{e}_{2} \), and \( \mathrm{e}_{3} \). Previous methods \( { }^{(9,10)} \)
which use the type \( C \) surface to partition viewing space
are not sufficient, because there are many event cells
that use this surface as their boundary. For instance, in
Fig. 15, we may see the transition between different
\( E E E \) events and the transition between the \( E E E \) event
and double- \( E E \) events are through the same \( E E E \)
singularity.
The double- \( E E \) event can be further divided into co
faced or non-cofaced double- \( E E \). The cofaced double
\( E E \) event requires that the two occluded edges be on the
same face. They may be the adjacent edges on the same
face that connect at the vertex. In this case, the
transition of the cofaced double- \( E E \) event is a so-called
type B surface. \( { }^{(10)} \) Previous methods \( { }^{(9,10)} \) which use the
type B surface to partition viewing space are sufficient
only for cofaced \( E E \) event transition. For non-cofaced
\( E E \) event transition, the type B surface is not adequate.
For a non-cofaced double- \( E E \) event, the two occluded
edges are not on the same faces which is much more
complex than the former one. These two edges may
generate the singularity by making one vertex enclose a
pseudo planar face with the enclosing edges coming
from different faces.
There are transitions between (1) \( E E E \) events; (2)
\( E E E \) event and double- \( E E \) event; (3) \( E E E \) event and
preceding- \( E E \) event; (4) \( E E E \) event and \( E E \) event; (5)
double- \( E E \) event and preceding- \( E E \) event; (6) double
\( E E \) event and \( E E \) event; and (7) preceding- \( E E \) event and
\( E E \) event. These transitions are through the EEE
singularities, foreground \( E E \) singularities, and back
ground \( E E \) singularities. The foreground singularity is
visible, whereas, the background singularity is invisible
which is basically a transition of the T junction of
\( \left(\mathrm{e}_{1}, \mathrm{e}_{3}\right) \) or \( \left(\mathrm{e}_{2}, \mathrm{e}_{3}\right) \). There is another foreground \( E E \)
singularity which causes the \( E E E \) event to degenerate
as a single \( E E \) event. We may summarize the transitions
between the \( E E E \) and \( E E \) events, illustrated in Fig. 15.
From a topological point of view, the aspect with the
type I \( E E E \) event and preceding- \( E E \) event will generate
the same aspect node. The same condition occurs for
the type II \( E E E \) event and double- \( E E \) event. Therefore,
the event cells are combined together as a single event
cell. In Fig. 15, we illustrate the different configurations
of \( E E \) events and \( E E E \) events that occur for three edges
( \( \mathrm{e}_{1}, \mathrm{e}_{2}, \mathrm{e}_{3} \) ). Type I \( E E E \) event to type II \( E E E \) event
transition must go through the \( E E E \) singularity,
whereas the transitions between the \( E E E \) event to the
\( E E \) event may go through either the \( E E E \) singularity or
the \( E E \) singularity (foreground or background). The
\( E E E \) singularity illustrates three edges are projected to
intercept at one single point.
The \( E E E \) event viewing cell finding algorithm is
based on the viewing subspace of the \( E E \) event. Actually,
the viewing cell of the \( E E E \) event is the intersection
space of the viewing subspace of \( E E \) events. Types
E-G are related to the occurrence of the \( E E E \) event. In
types A-D, we consider the interception between two
edges. The visibility of the background edge is our
concern. The background edge may be visible or in
visible. Besides the visibility of the background edge,
we find that the T-junction formed by the foreground
edge and the background edge also has the visibility
problem. Though there is no interception between
these two edges, there may be interception between
viewpoint and foreground.
3.1. EEE event generated by the interception outside
tetrahedron
In Section 2, we introduced the edge pair categoriz
ation, and discussed the interception types A-D.
Types E-I are related to the occurrence of the EEE
event. In the following, types E-I will be discussed.
Type E. Type E indicates that there is no interception
outside the tetrahedron. Type E is often found for
simple objects of which the faces are not intercepted by
the boundary edges of the viewing subspace. In Fig. 16,
the half lines ce, cf, dh, and dg do not go through any
face. The resulting viewing cell will be the same as the
original viewing subspace.
Type F. Type F is the case that there is an intercep
tion between the viewpoint and foreground edge. Type
F only exists for occlusion type II or type IV (see Table
3). The necessary condition for the existence of type \( F \) is
that the occlusion number of the foreground edge is 2 .
In Fig. 17, there is no interception between the two
edges ( ab and cd), however, the AP (b,c,d) is intercepted
outside the tetrahedron. If the viewpoint is located in
V1, the T-junction is visible. But when the viewpoint is
located in V2, the viewing direction will be intercepted
by face C . So, the viewing sub-cell from which the
T-junction is invisible must be pruned from the original
viewing subspace. In Fig. 18 region I is the original
viewing subspace and region II is the to-be-removed
region which shall be removed from the original
viewing subspace. In type F, we may observe double
\( E E \) events (Fig. 15). There are another three cases for
type F (see Fig. 18): (a) if \( \theta_{1} \) is larger than \( \theta_{\text {II }} \) the resulting
viewing subspace is obtained by subtracting region II
from region I; (b) when \( \theta_{\text {II }} \) increases and becomes equal
to \( \theta_{\mathrm{I}} \), the viewing subspace disappears; (c) if \( \theta_{\mathrm{II}} \) is even
larger, region II will contain region I and the viewing
subspace does not exist, it becomes type H .
Fig. 16. An example of type E .
Fig. 17. (a) An example of type F . (b) The top view.
Fig. 18. Other cases of type F.
Type G. Type G indicates that the viewing subspace
is partially intercepted by multiple faces and the \( E E E \)
event is generated. The type I-III \( E E \) events and
preceding-EE event may occur in type G. In Fig. 19, the
half line ac intersects face A at pt 1 and face B at pt2. The
half line ad intersects face A at pt3 and face B at pt4.
Half line bc and half line bd do not intersect any face.
Faces A and B are the intercepting faces. The original
viewing subspace of edges 1 and 2 is intercepted by
faces A and B . The viewing cell finding algorithm for
Fig. 19. An example of type G.
type G is:
Step 1. Find the edge pair, part of them are located
in the original viewing subspace, i.e. in Fig. 19, we find
edge A and edge B.
Step 2. Build the planes which are ( \( \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{e}, \mathrm{f}),(\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{h}, \mathrm{g}) \),
(d, e,f), and (d,h,g). The inside part of those planes are
the vertices of face A except h and g.
Step 3. Union the inside part of those planes and the
subspace is called cut subspace.
Step 4. Remove the cut subspace from the original
viewing subspace.
Type H. Type H indicates that the T-junction is
invisible because between the viewpoint and the fore
ground edge there exists a single-face complete inter
ception. Type H is an extreme case of type F. From
Fig. 20, the background edge 1 is located in the outside
part of faces \( \mathbf{A} \) and \( \mathbf{B} \). The \( E E \) event for cd and ab is
completely occluded. The necessary condition of oc-
Fig. 20. An example of type H.
currence for type H is that the occluding number of the
foreground edge is 2 .
Type I. Type I indicates that there is multi-face
complete interception between the viewpoint and
foreground edge. In Fig. 21, the original viewing sub
space for the \( E E \) event generated by ed1 and ed2 is
completely intercepted by a sheet. The half lines ac, ad,
bc , and bd are intercepted by faces A and B that makes
the T-junction invisible.
3.2. Relation between viewing cells of \( E E E \) event and \( E E \)
event
There may be some viewing cells of \( E E E \) events
existing in one viewing subspace of the \( E E \) event. The
\( E E E \) event occurs when the viewing subspace of one \( E E \)
event overlaps with the viewing subspace of another
\( E E \) event. Figure 22 shows an example for the viewing
subspaces of \( E E \) events. Region \( A \) is the viewing
subspace of the \( E E_{1,2} \) event and region \( B \) the viewing
subspace of the \( E E_{2,3} \) event. Region C is the intersection
region of regions A and B , and it represents the viewing
cell of the \( E E E_{1,2,3} \) event (el is the background edge,
e2 the middle edge, and e3 the foreground edge). Not
all intersecting viewing subspaces of \( E E \) events will
produce an \( E E E \) event. The necessary conditions of an
\( E E E \) event are: (1) the viewing subspaces of two \( E E \)
events must have an intersection region; (2) the fore
ground or the background edges of one \( E E \) event and
the background or the foreground edges of the other
\( E E \) event are the same edge. The viewing cell of an \( E E E \)
event is described as the intersection of two or three \( E E \)
event cells:
where \( R_{E E E} \) and \( R_{E E} \) are the viewing cells of \( E E E \) and
Fig. 21. An example of type I.
Fig. 22. The relation of the viewing subspace between \( E E \) and \( E E E \) events.
\( E E \) events. In Fig. 22, regions A and C comprise the
viewing cell of the \( E E_{1,2} \) event and regions \( B \) and \( C \) the
viewing cell of the \( E E_{2,3} \) event. The boundary "ab" is
called the \( E E \) event when the viewpoint passes from the
viewing cell of the \( E E_{1,2} \) event to the viewing cell of the
\( E E E_{1,2,3} \) event. But if the viewpoint passes from the
viewing cell of the \( E E E_{1,2,3} \) event to the viewing cell
of the \( E E_{1,2} \) event, the boundary "ab" is called the
4. ALGORITHMS
Given all the parcellation planes above, we can
partition the viewing space into the event cell for that
particular event. Every aspect is composed of a different
combination of events. For a certain aspect, the
corresponding aspect cell is intersection of the re
spective event cells. If the intersection of the respective
event cell exist, the aspect can be perceived from the
viewpoints in that aspect cell. The aspect is composed
of different combinations of features: (1) a number of
the visible faces; (2) the total number of visible faces; (3)
a number of \( E E \) events for the partially occluded faces;
(4) the positions of the \( E E \) events on the respective face;
(5) the number of \( E E E \) events for two occluded faces; (6)
the positions of the \( E E E \) events on the respective two
faces. To test all the possible intersections of all the
event cells is very complicated and computation ex
pensive. The alternative way to find the possible
intersections is through the test points. The test points
are found to cover all the possible existing aspect cells.
From the test point validation process, we may find all
the existing aspect cells.
In this section, we address the algorithms for aspect
graph generation. Firstly, we illustrate the \( E E \) event
types classification algorithm which consists of 14
procedures to categorize different interception types
(types A-I) and generate the coefficients which describe
the viewing subspace for each edge type. Secondly, we
propose the structure of our aspect graph generation
system. Here, we show the procedures which differen
tiate interception types of \( E E \) events. In the following
paragraph, denotations such as \( t_{k}, P_{k} \), and half lines
(da, db, ca, cb) are adopted from the \( E E \) event finding
criteria.
Procedure 1
(a) Use the possible existence testing criteria to
check the edge pair.
(b) If it is, continue, otherwise, stop.
Procedure 2
(a) Use the single-face complete interception testing
criteria to check the edge pair.
(b) If it is, stop. Otherwise, continue.
Procedure 3
Construct a tetrahedron bounded by four faces
which are generated by connecting the four edge
terminals.
Procedure 4
Build the original viewing subspace using \( E E \) event
viewing subspace finding criteria.
Procedure 5
(a) Check the line segments \( \mathrm{ac}, \mathrm{bc}, \mathrm{ad} \), and bd . If \( t_{k} \)
fits conditions \( a \) and \( b \) (refer to equation (6)), then make
it type A. Otherwise, go to Procedure 6.
(b) Determine the viewing subspace of the \( E E \) event
by the original viewing subspace which is bounded by
the four half-planes extended from the four planar sides
of the tetrahedron. Go to Procedure 10.
Procedure 6
Find the interception relations between the four
boundary edges of the tetrahedron and all the faces of
the object. Check one of the following three cases:
(a) At least one, but not all of the boundary edges of
the tetrahedron are intercepted by a single face. Make it
type B, then go to Procedure 7.
(b) At least one, but not all of the boundary edges of
the tetrahedron are intercepted by several faces simul
taneously. Make it type \( C \), then go to Procedure 8.
(c) All the boundary edges of the tetrahedron are
intercepted by several faces. Make it type \( D \), then go to
Procedure 9.
Procedure 7
(a) Select one of the intersecting faces of the back
ground edge of which the BPs intercept the foreground
edge.
(b) Prune the inside part of the BP away from the
original viewing subspace.
(c) Assign the remaining viewing subspace for the
type B EE event. Go to Procedure 10.
Procedure 8
(a) Find the boundary edges of interception faces
which are partially located in the tetrahedron.
(b) Generate the to-be-removed subspace by con
necting the four terminals of the background edge and
the edges obtained in step (a).
(c) Prune the to-be-removed subspace away from
the original viewing subspace and generate the viewing
subspace of the type CEE event. Go to Procedure 10.
Procedure 9
(a) Generate no viewing subspace for the type \( D \)
edge pair, and stop.
Procedure 10
If half lines extended from db, da, cb, and ca do not
intersect any faces outside the tetrahedron, then let the
viewing subspace be determined by the pruned viewing
subspace, and stop. Otherwise, continue.
Procedure 11
Find the relation between the boundary half lines of
the original viewing subspace and all the faces of the
object. Check one of the following four cases:
(a) Part of the boundary half lines are intercepted by
one face. Make it type F, go to Procedure 12.
(b) Part of the boundary half lines are intercepted by
several faces. Make it type G, go to Procedure 13.
(c) All the boundary half lines are intercepted by one
face. Make it type \( H \), go to Procedure 14.
(d) All the boundary half lines are intercepted by
several faces. Make it type \( I \), go to Procedure 14.
Procedure 12
(a) Select one of the intersecting faces of the back
ground edge of which the expanding plane crosses the
foreground edge.
(b) Find the intersection of two subspaces from the
inside parts of the intersecting faces selected in step (a);
the intersection subspace is the to-be-removed subspace
(c) Clip the to-be-removed subspace away from the
pruned viewing subspace and generate the genuine
viewing subspace for the type FEE event, then stop.
Procedure 13
(a) Search their boundary edges of the interception
faces, and find the edges that are partially or completely
located inside the original viewing subspace.
(b) Get the new viewing subspace through handling
the foreground edge and one of the edges, and the third
edge obtained in step (a). The viewing subspace can be
obtained through finding the intersection of two sub
spaces: the original pruned viewing subspace; and the
viewing subspace of the new edge pair. (The new
foreground edge is the third edge and the new back
ground edge is the original foreground edge.)
Procedure 14
(a) If the interception face or faces are found, there is
no viewing subspace generated for the edge pair.
(b) Generate an empty viewing subspace for type \( H \)
and type I edge pairs, and then stop.
Our system for aspect graph creation operates in five
stages.
Stage (a). Use the definitions of the planar faces, the
\( E E \) events, as well as the \( E E E \) events to create the
"parcellation graph" which completely describes the
parcellation of space surrounding the objects into
viewing cells which capture the corresponding faces,
\( E E \) events, or \( E E E \) events. The facial node contains the
coefficients generated by the method mentioned in
Section 2.1. The \( E E \) node contains the coefficients
generated by \( E E \) event types classification algorithm.
Stage (b). Employ the parcellation graph to create a
"face-level enumeration array" which assembles the
facial nodes of the top level of the parcellation graph.
The algorithm also generates the "edge-level enumera
tion array" which assembles the \( E E \) nodes of the second
level and the \( E E E \) nodes of the third level of the
parcellation graph.
Stage (c). Utilize the multiple Gaussian spheres to
generate the test points.
Stage (d). Use the face-level and edge-level enu
meration arrays to construct the visibility numbers for
plausible aspect graph nodes. The plausible aspect
node is composed of all combinations of the parcellation
nodes with different visibility nodes. The test points are
used to identify the visibility of the nodes in the parcel
lation graph. If it is visible then the corresponding
position of the visibility number is encoded with one,
otherwise, it is encoded with zero.
Stage(e). Generate an explicit aspect graph structure.
The input to our algorithm is a standard boundary
surface representation of a non-convex planar-face
object. Each face is specified by a planar polygon, and
each polygon is specified by an order list of vertices.
The vertices around the edge of a face are specified in
counter-clockwise order as seen from outside the
object. This allows us to easily and unambiguously
refer to the "inside" and "outside" of each plane which
contains a face of an object. The detailed implementa
tion of our system is illustrated as follows.
(a) Parcellation graph generation
The first step in creating the aspect graph is to
generate the intermediate parcellation graph data
structure. The parcellation graph is a three-level bi
directional graph which summarizes the parcellation of
space by generating the nodes that indicate the facial
events, the \( E E \) events, and the \( E E E \) events. (1) Level I
parcellation. The \( N \) possible facial events which are
expanding planes (BP) in which convex (non-occluding)
faces of the object lie that do not intercept any other
faces and intersecting half-planes in which concave
(occluding) faces may not be occluded. The convex
faces may either be visible or invisible, whereas the
concave faces may be partially occluded. (2) Level II
parcellation. The \( E^{2} \) possible \( E E \) events in which the
\( 4 E^{2} \) likely half-planes generated by the four terminals
of any possible two occluding edges of the objects.
(3) Level III parcellation. The \( E^{3} \) plausible \( E E E \) events
in which the \( 12 E^{3} \) plausible half-planes generated by
the six terminals of three occluding edges of the object
lines. The nodes of the parcellation graph are composed
of Names and Attributes. Note, however, that for a
given set of \( E \) edges, the existing level II nodes will
always be less than \( E^{2} \) plausible \( E E \) events and the
surviving level III nodes are even much less than \( E^{3} \)
possible \( E E E \) events. There are intra-level links as well
as inter-level links.
At the second level of the parcellation graph are
nodes for all the possible \( E E \) events of the two edges of
two non-neighboring faces. The inter-level link between
the nodes of the first two levels indicate the relationship
of the face and its bounding edges. The Name of the
node indicates foreground-background edges and the
Attribute of the node is the extended tetrahedron
enclosed by four half-planes and the bounded tetra
hedron surrounded by four triangles formed by the
four terminals of the two edges. The Attribute indicates
the possible viewing cell that may capture the occlusion
event of the two edges. The level II nodes are identified
by non-interception \( E E \) event finding criteria. If the
level II nodes have the same foreground edge, then they
are linked to the same level III node. The two back
ground edges of those level II nodes may also make
occlusion with each other and create other \( E E E \) events.
This level III node, therefore, may have inter-level links
to three level II nodes. At the third level of the
parcellation graph are nodes for all the possible \( E E E \)
events of three edges of three non-adjacent faces. The
relation between \( E E \) nodes and \( E E E \) nodes will be
generated after step (d). Figure 23 shows the structure
of the parcellation graph.
(b) Generation of enumeration arrays
Suppose we have a total of \( M \) nodes generated in the
parcellation graph. We may identify the plausible
nodes of the aspect graph and their corresponding
aspect cells of space using \( M \)-bit numbers. Each bit
corresponds to one of the \( M \) possible events. A bit value
of " 1 " indicates that the corresponding event can be
observed. This numbering scheme allows for the speci
fication of any of the \( 2^{M} \) possible combinations of
relationships to the \( M \) events. Note that the number of
Fig. 23. The parcellation graph.
Fig. 24. The face-level enumeration array and the edge-level enumeration array.
existing real aspect graph nodes will be always less than
\( 2^{M} \) plausible combinations. The algorithm will deter
mine which of the \( 2^{M} \) plausible kinds of the aspect cells
may exit.
In stage two, we create the "face-level enumeration
array" which describes the relationship between the
facial nodes and the test point. The "edge-level enu
meration array" which describes the relation between
the nodes of \( E E, E E E \) events and the test point. The
plausible aspect cell identification number determines
the relationship of the test points and the enumerate
arrays. Figure 24 shows the face-level enumeration
array and edge-level enumeration array.
(c) Generation of test points
For creating the test points, we use multiple Gaussian
spheres, which have their common center located on
the centroids of viewed objects, to generate a set of test
points. The Gaussian spheres with different radius are
used for generating the test points which are evenly
distributed on the spheres. We let the number of
test points on each Gaussian sphere be the same. In
other words, for each small solid angle from the
centroid of the object, there are a set of test points with
different distances from the object. The density of these
test points is determined by the complexity of the
object, as long as no aspect is missed between two test
points. In Fig. 25, we illustrate the Gaussian sphere
from which the test points are selected for a polyhedron.
(d) Generation of plausible aspect cell
The aspect cell identification number specifies a set
of relationships to the \( M \) event cells if and only if we can
find at least one test point lying in the interior of the
aspect cell. A valid aspect cell identification number
indicates a combination of some specific events that are
physically observable from the corresponding test
point. We need to cope with the following two problems:
(1) selecting a small set of test points which cover the
viewing space surrounding the object sufficiently to
validate all existing aspect cells no matter how small
they are; (2) determining the boundary description
of existing aspect cells. In this stage, the algorithm
generates the plausible aspect cells by enumerating
their corresponding identification numbers. The algor
ithm recursively examines the face-level enumer
ate array and edge-level enumerate array to con
struct plausible node identification numbers which are
Fig. 25. Test points on Gaussian sphere.
illustrated in the following steps:
Step 1. Input a test point.
Step 2. Take the face-level enumerate array, and
enter the identification number for each node. The
identification number is 1 if the test point is located in
the viewing subspace of the node, and is 0 if it is not.
Step 3. From the face-level enumerate array, check
if its identification number is 0 (i.e. the test point is in the
outside part of the BP of the facial node).
Step 4. If "YES", continue, else try another face and
go to step 6.
Step 5. For this facial node, find the identification
number of the edge-level enumerate array. (This step
only tests the existence of \( E E \) nodes whose background
edge is the boundary edge of this facial node.)
Step 6. If the facial node is the last node, then go to
step 7 , otherwise take the next facial node, go to step 3 .
Step 7. The combination of the face-level enumerate
array and the edge-level enumerate array indicates a
plausible aspect node. Check if the plausible aspect
node already exists. If "YES", ignore it. Otherwise,
make it another plausible aspect node.
Step 8. If there is another test point untested, go to
step 1, otherwise stop.
(e) Aspect graph creation
We have allocated every node and its attribute with
the identification number and the cell boundary
description. The identification number is a link to
the definitions of the visible events. Two nodes are
Fig. 26. (a) Block 1 and two test viewpoints. (b) Parcellation graph of block 1. (c) The identified face-level
and edge-level enumeration array for viewpoints.
considered to be adjacent if their identification numbers
differ in only a single bit position. For any two nodes
that have only one bit difference in their identification
number, we create a link between them.
Figure 26 shows an example of block 1.
5. EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS
In this section, we will investigate nine blocks and
show some aspects for each case. The nine blocks show
some aspects for five cases. Blocks 1, 7 , and 9 have been
discussed in the preceding sections. The other blocks
are new cases. Figure 27 shows the cases of blocks 1-9.
In Table 4, we show the existence of different edge
types of the nine blocks (i.e. characteristic table)
separately and examine the experimental results. Table
5 shows the dimension of the face-level enumeration
array and edge-level enumeration array, and the count
of the aspects corresponding to each block.
In Fig. 28, we illustrate some aspects found for five
blocks-blocks 3, 5, 6, 8, and 9.
6. CONCLUSION
We have presented an algorithm for generating the
exact perspective projection aspect graph of general

polyhedra from their boundary surface description.
We believe that the aspect graph is a robust and useful
tool for machine vision and the perspective projection
for convex as well as non-convex polyhedra is applicable.
We apply our algorithm to various different objects
and the shapes of those objects are not regular. The
faces of the object may be a triangle or a polygon, also
they may be inclined. In this paper, we strictly analyze
all the relations between each edge pair and classify all
the edge pairs into several edge types. We use occlusion
type, interception type, and group 1 or group 2 to
classify the edge pairs. Through the edge type categor
ization, all the important aspects of polyhedra will be
generated. We can handle more complex non-convex
polyhedra. Furthermore, we also investigate the \( E E E \)
event, the occurrence condition and the transition
between \( E E E \) events.
However, we only consider those viewpoints which
are located outside the object boundary. When the
viewer moves inside the concave portions of the object,
the aspects will depend on not only the viewpoints but
also the viewing directions. From the same viewpoint,
the viewer may look in many different directions and
see several different aspects. We do not consider this
case in this paper. We plan to generalize our algorithm
to handle more complicated polyhedral objects or
curve shaped objects.

About the Author-I-Cheng Chang was born in Taipei, Taiwan, in 1965. He received his B.S. degree from
Nuclear Engineering Department, National Tsing-Hua University, in 1987 and his M.S. from Electrical
Engineering Department, National Tsing-Hua University, in June 1991. Currently, he is a Ph.D. student in
the Electrical Engineering Department, Tsing-Hua University. His research interests are computer vision
and image processing.
About the Author-Chung-Lin Huang was born in Taichung, Taiwan, in 1955. He received his B.S. degree
in nuclear engineering from National Tsing-Hua University, Hsin-Chu, Taiwan, in 1977, and M.S. degree
in electrical engineering from National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan, in 1979, respectively. He obtained
his Ph.D. degree in electrical engineering from University of Florida, Gainesville, Florida, U.S.A., in 1987.
From 1981 to 1983, he was an associate engineer in ERSO, ITRI, Hsin-Chu, Taiwan. From 1987 to 1988,
he worked for the Unisys Co., Orange County, California, U.S.A., as a project engineer. Since August 1988
he has been with the Electrical Engineering Department, National Tsing-Hua University as an associate
professor. His research interests are in the areas of image processing, computer vision, and visual
communication. Dr Huang is a member of IEEE and SPIE.
